content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 2 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 266-267 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1PE_002_page_001.jpg, 1PE_002_page_002.jpg

1. Therefore, putting away all malice and all deceit and hypocrisy and envy and all slander,

2. as newborn infants, long for the pure spiritual milk, that by it you may grow into salvation,

3. if indeed you have tasted that the Lord is good.

4. Coming to him, a living stone rejected by men but in the sight of God chosen and precious,

5. you yourselves also, as living stones, are being built up as a spiritual house into a holy priesthood, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ.

6. For it stands in Scripture: "Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone, a cornerstone chosen and precious, and whoever believes in him shall not be put to shame."

7. To you therefore who believe belongs the honor, but for those who disbelieve, "The stone that the builders rejected, this one has become the head of the corner,"

8. and "A stone of stumbling and a rock of offense." They stumble because they disobey the word, to which they were also appointed.

9. But you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession, that you may proclaim the excellencies of him who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light.

10. You who once were not a people but now are the people of God; who had not received mercy but now have received mercy.

11. Beloved, I urge you as sojourners and exiles to abstain from the passions of the flesh, which wage war against the soul.

12. Keep your conduct among the nations honorable, so that when they speak against you as evildoers, they may see your good works and glorify God on the day of visitation.

13. Be subject for the Lord's sake to every human institution, whether to the emperor as supreme,

14. or to governors as sent through him for the punishment of evildoers and the praise of those who do good.

15. For such is the will of God, that by doing good you should silence the ignorance of foolish people.

16. Live as free people, yet not using your freedom as a covering for evil, but as servants of God.

17. Honor all people. Love the brotherhood. Fear God. Honor the emperor.

18. Servants, be subject to your masters with all fear, not only to the good and gentle but also to the crooked.

19. For this is a grace, if on account of consciousness of God anyone bears sorrows while suffering unjustly.

20. For what credit is it if, when you sin and are beaten, you endure? But if when you do good and suffer, you endure, this is a grace before God.

21. For to this you were called, because Christ also suffered for you, leaving you an example, that you should follow in his steps.

22. He committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth.

23. When he was reviled, he did not revile in return; when he suffered, he did not threaten, but kept entrusting himself to the one who judges justly.

24. He himself bore our sins in his body upon the tree, that we, having died to sins, might live to righteousness. By his wounds you were healed.

25. For you were going astray like sheep, but you have now returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"Pure spiritual milk" (v.2):** The Greek τὸ λογικὸν ἄδολον γάλα presents a translation challenge. λογικόν can mean "rational," "spiritual," or even "of the word" (connecting to λόγος). Sinaiticus reads λογικον clearly. The formal rendering "spiritual" preserves the contrast with physical nourishment while pointing to the metaphorical dimension of the infant imagery. The adjective ἄδολον ("unadulterated, without deceit") echoes the δόλον ("deceit") rejected in v.1 — the milk must be free of the very vice believers are to discard.

2. **The stone testimonies (vv.6-8):** Peter weaves together three Old Testament passages: Isaiah 28:16 (the precious cornerstone, v.6), Psalm 118:22 (the rejected stone becoming the head of the corner, v.7), and Isaiah 8:14 (the stone of stumbling, v.8). This catena of stone texts was likely a pre-existing Christian collection (a "testimony book"), as the same combination appears in Romans 9:32-33. Sinaiticus preserves the standard text here with minor orthographic variations.

3. **"A chosen race, a royal priesthood" (v.9):** Peter applies to the church four titles drawn from Exodus 19:5-6 and Isaiah 43:20-21, originally designating Israel. The transfer of these covenant designations to a mixed Jewish-Gentile community is theologically momentous. The Sinaiticus text reads βασιλειον ιερατευμα ("royal priesthood") following the LXX of Exodus 19:6, rather than the alternative "kingdom of priests."

4. **"Consciousness of God" (v.19):** The Sinaiticus text reads συνειδησιν θεου, rendered formally as "consciousness of God." This is not merely "conscience" in the modern psychological sense but an awareness of God that shapes one's response to unjust suffering. The word συνείδησις in Petrine usage carries the force of an orientation of the whole person toward God.

5. **"For you" not "for us" (v.21):** Sinaiticus reads ὑπὲρ ὑμῶν ("for you") rather than the later Byzantine reading ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν ("for us"). This second-person form maintains the direct pastoral address to the audience, consistent with the paraenetic tone of the passage. The shift to "you" makes the suffering of Christ immediately personal to the readers' own situation of unjust suffering described in vv.18-20.

6. **"Upon the tree" (v.24):** The phrase ἐπὶ τὸ ξύλον ("upon the tree") echoes Deuteronomy 21:22-23, where hanging on a tree signifies divine curse. Peter, like Paul in Galatians 3:13, draws on this deuteronomic theology: Christ bore the curse-bearing death so that believers might live to righteousness. The verse also alludes to Isaiah 53:4-5, 12, completing the Servant Song typology that runs through vv.21-25.

7. **Household code context (vv.13-17, 18-25):** The instruction to servants (οἰκέται, v.18) is part of a household code (Haustafeln) that extends into chapter 3. Peter does not endorse the institution of slavery but addresses believers within the social structures of the Roman Empire. The theological grounding is remarkable: the unjust suffering of enslaved believers is patterned after Christ's own suffering, transforming their social degradation into a participation in the paradigmatic story of redemption.

-- LOGOS, AI Translator
